subject: Physics
q:
[12]
A solid ball of radius $\mathrm{R}$ has a charge density $\rho$ given by $\rho=\rho_0\left(1-\frac{r}{R}\right)$ for $0 \leq r \leq R$. The electric field outside the ball is:
question:
[12]
A solid ball of radius $\mathrm{R}$ has a charge density $\rho$ given by $\rho=\rho_0\left(1-\frac{r}{R}\right)$ for $0 \leq r \leq R$. The electric field outside the ball is:
solution:
Charge density, $\rho=\rho_0\left(1-\frac{r}{R}\right)$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&d q=\rho d v \\
&q_{\text {in }}=\int d q=\rho d v \\
&=\rho_0\left(1-\frac{r}{R}\right) 4 \pi r^2 d r \quad\left(\because d v=4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2 \mathrm{dr}\right) \\
&=4 \pi \rho_0 \int_0^R\left(1-\frac{r}{R}\right) r^2 d r \\
&=4 \pi \rho_0 \int_0^R r^2 d r-\frac{r^2}{R} d r \\
&=4 \pi \rho_0\left[\left[\frac{r^3}{3}\right]_0^R-\left[\frac{r^4}{4 R}\right]_0^R\right] \\
&=4 \pi \rho_0\left[\frac{R^3}{3}-\frac{R^4}{4 R}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$
$=4 \pi \rho_0\left[\frac{R^3}{3}-\frac{R^3}{4}\right]=4 \pi \rho_0\left[\frac{R^3}{12}\right]$
$q=\frac{\pi \rho_0 R^3}{3}$

$$
E .4 \pi r^2=\left(\frac{\pi \rho_0 R^3}{3 \epsilon_0}\right)
$$
$\therefore \quad$ Electric field outside the ball, $E=\frac{\rho_0 R^3}{12 \in_0 r^2}$

question: A cube is placed inside an electric field, E→=150y2j^ The side of the cube is 0.5 m and is placed in the field as shown in the given figure. The charge inside the cube is:
solution:
By gauss law ∮E→·ds→=qin ε0

ε0(150)(0.5)2×(0.5)2=qin

qin=8.85×10-12×15016=8.3×10-11C

subject: Physics
q: A point charge of magnitude $+1 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is fixed at $(0$, $0,0)$. An isolated uncharged spherical conductor, is fixed with its center at $(4,0,0)$. The potential and the induced electric field at the centre of the sphere is :
question: A point charge of magnitude $+1 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is fixed at $(0$, $0,0)$. An isolated uncharged spherical conductor, is fixed with its center at $(4,0,0)$. The potential and the induced electric field at the centre of the sphere is :
solution:
$\mathrm{q}=1 \mu \mathrm{C}=1 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C}$
$$
\mathrm{r}=4 \mathrm{~cm}=4 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}
$$
Potential $\mathrm{V}=\frac{\mathrm{kq}}{\mathrm{r}}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 10^{-2}} \\
& =2.25 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~V} .
\end{aligned}
$$
Induced electric field $\mathrm{E}=-\frac{\mathrm{kq}}{\mathrm{r}^2}$
$$
=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{16 \times 10^{-4}}=-5.625 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}
$$

subject: Physics
q: The flat base of a hemisphere of radius a with no charge inside it lies in a horizontal plane. A uniform electric field $\vec{E}$ is applied at an angle $\frac{\pi}{4}$ with the vertical direction. The electric flux through the curved surface of the hemisphere is
question: The flat base of a hemisphere of radius a with no charge inside it lies in a horizontal plane. A uniform electric field $\vec{E}$ is applied at an angle $\frac{\pi}{4}$ with the vertical direction. The electric flux through the curved surface of the hemisphere is
solution:
We know that,
$$
\phi=\oint E \cdot d S=E \oint d S \cos 45^{\circ}
$$
In case of hemisphere
$$
\phi_{\text {curved }}=\phi_{\text {circular }}
$$
Therefore, $\phi_{\text {curved }}=E \pi a^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{E \pi a^2}{\sqrt{2}}$

subject: Physics
q:
Identify the correct statements :
A. Electrostatic field lines form closed loops.
B. The electric field lines point radially outward when charge is greater than zero.
C. The Gauss - Law is valid only for inverse - square force.
D. The workdone in moving a charged particle in a static electric field around a closed path is zero.
E. The motion of a particle under Coulomb's force must take place in a plane.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Identify the correct statements :
A. Electrostatic field lines form closed loops.
B. The electric field lines point radially outward when charge is greater than zero.
C. The Gauss - Law is valid only for inverse - square force.
D. The workdone in moving a charged particle in a static electric field around a closed path is zero.
E. The motion of a particle under Coulomb's force must take place in a plane.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Statement A: Electrostatic field lines originate from positive charges and terminate on negative charges. They do not form closed loops because the electrostatic field is conservative. Thus, A is incorrect.
Statement B: For a positive point charge ($q > 0$), the electric field lines are directed radially outwards. Thus, B is correct.
Statement C: Gauss's Law is a consequence of the inverse-square nature of Coulomb's law ($F \propto 1/r^2$). If the force law were different, Gauss's Law would not hold in its standard form. Thus, C is correct.
Statement D: The electrostatic field is conservative, meaning the work done in moving a charge along any closed path is zero ($\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$). Thus, D is correct.
Statement E: Coulomb's force is a central force. The motion of a particle under a central force always takes place in a plane because the torque about the center is zero, keeping angular momentum constant in direction. Thus, E is correct.
Since B, C, D, and E are correct, the correct option is (4).

q:
This question contains Statement-1 and Statement-2. Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
Statement - 1: For a charged particle moving from point $P$ to point $Q$, the net work done by an electrostatic field on the particle is independent of the path connecting point $P$ to point $Q$.
Statement-2: The net work done by a conservative force on an object moving along a closed loop is zero
question:
This question contains Statement-1 and Statement-2. Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
Statement - 1: For a charged particle moving from point $P$ to point $Q$, the net work done by an electrostatic field on the particle is independent of the path connecting point $P$ to point $Q$.
Statement-2: The net work done by a conservative force on an object moving along a closed loop is zero
options: Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is false
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is not the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is false, Statement-2 is true
solution: Work done by conservative force does not depend on the path. Electrostatic force is a conservative force.

subject: Physics
q: An electric field, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{6}}$ passes through the surface of $4 \mathrm{~m}^2$ area having unit vector $\hat{n}=\left(\frac{2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{6}}\right)$. The electric flux for that surface is ______ $\mathrm{Vm}$.
question: An electric field, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{6}}$ passes through the surface of $4 \mathrm{~m}^2$ area having unit vector $\hat{n}=\left(\frac{2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{6}}\right)$. The electric flux for that surface is ______ $\mathrm{Vm}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} \phi & =\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}} \\ & =\left(\frac{2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+8 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{6}}\right) \cdot 4\left(\frac{2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{6}}\right) \\ & =\frac{4}{6} \times(4+6+8)=12 \mathrm{Vm}\end{aligned}$

q: An electric charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is placed at origin <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> co-ordinate system. Two points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> are situated at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msqrt><mn>6</mn></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> respectively. The potential difference between the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> will be :
question: An electric charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is placed at origin <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> co-ordinate system. Two points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> are situated at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msqrt><mn>6</mn></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> respectively. The potential difference between the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> will be :
solution:
The formula to calculate the potential difference between P and Q is given by

∆V=kq1r1-1r2   ...1

Now,

r1=(3)2+(3)2 m=6 m

And,

r2=(6)2+0 m=6 m

As r1=r2=6 m and the value of the charge remains constant, the potential difference between the points is zero.

q: Two charges of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μC</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μC</mi></math> are placed at the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>5</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> located in an electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The magnitude of the torque acting on the dipole is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><msqrt><mi>α</mi></msqrt><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo></math>_________.
question: Two charges of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μC</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μC</mi></math> are placed at the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>5</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> located in an electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The magnitude of the torque acting on the dipole is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><msqrt><mi>α</mi></msqrt><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo></math>_________.
solution:
Given the electric field is

E→=0.20 i^ Vcm=20i^ Vm

The formula for calculating the torque is

τ→=p→×E→   ...1

where,

p→=ql→=4×2i^+-1j^+5k^-1i^+0j^+4k^=4i^-j^+k^ μC m

Thus, from equation (1), it follows that

τ→=(4i^-4j^+4k^)×10-6×(20i^) N m=(8k^+8j^)×10-5 N m⇒τ→=82+82×10-5 N m=82×10-5 N m

Hence, α=2.

subject: Physics
q: The surface charge density of a thin charged disc of radius $\mathrm{R}$ is $\sigma$. The value of the electric field at the centre of the disc is $\frac{\sigma}{2 \in_0}$. With respect to the field at the centre, the electric field along the axis at a distance $\mathrm{R}$ from the centre of the disc:
question: The surface charge density of a thin charged disc of radius $\mathrm{R}$ is $\sigma$. The value of the electric field at the centre of the disc is $\frac{\sigma}{2 \in_0}$. With respect to the field at the centre, the electric field along the axis at a distance $\mathrm{R}$ from the centre of the disc:
solution:
Electric field intensity at the centre of the disc.
$$
\mathrm{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_0} \quad \text { (given) }
$$
Electric field along the axis at any distance $x$ from the centre of the disc
$$
\mathrm{E}^{\prime}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_0}\left(1-\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{R}^2}}\right)
$$
From question, $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{R}$ (radius of disc)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \mathrm{E}^{\prime} &=\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_0}\left(1-\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{R}^2}}\right) \\
&=\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_0}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2} \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{R}}{\sqrt{2} \mathrm{R}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
=\frac{4}{14} \mathrm{E}
$$
$\therefore \%$ reduction in the value of electric field
$$
=\frac{\left(E-\frac{4}{14} E\right) \times 100}{E}=\frac{1000}{14} \%=70.7 \%
$$

= 4KQ π R 2

E→net=25×103 N C-1 i^

subject: Physics
q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo> </mo></math> charge is divided into two parts and placed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> distance so that the repulsive force between them is maximum. The charges of the two parts are:
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo> </mo></math> charge is divided into two parts and placed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> distance so that the repulsive force between them is maximum. The charges of the two parts are:
solution:
Let the charges be x μC, q-x μC, where q=10 μC

The force between them is 

F=Kx(q-x)r2.

For the force to be maximum,

dFdx=0⇒dFdx=K(q-2x)r2=0⇒x=q2=5 μC

subject: Physics
q: A certain charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is divided into two parts <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>Q</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>.</mo></math> How should the charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> be divided so that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>Q</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo></math> placed at a certain distance apart experience maximum electrostatic repulsion?
question: A certain charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is divided into two parts <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>Q</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>.</mo></math> How should the charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> be divided so that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>Q</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo></math> placed at a certain distance apart experience maximum electrostatic repulsion?
solution:
Fq=kq(Q-q)L2=kL2qQ-q2
dFdq=0 when force is maximum
dFdq=kL2[Q-2q]=0
⇒Q-2q=0⇒Q=2q

subject: Physics
q: Five positive charges each having charge $q$ are placed at the vertices of a pentagon as shown in the figure. The electric potential $(V)$ and the electric field $(\vec{E})$ at the center $O$ of the pentagon due to these five positive charges are:
question: Five positive charges each having charge $q$ are placed at the vertices of a pentagon as shown in the figure. The electric potential $(V)$ and the electric field $(\vec{E})$ at the center $O$ of the pentagon due to these five positive charges are:
solution:
The electric potential at center O is the scalar sum of contributions from all 5 charges.
Each charge is equidistant from center, so $V = 5 cdot \frac{kq}{r} = \frac{5q}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r}$.
The electric field is a vector sum of field vectors from all 5 charges.
Due to 5-fold rotational symmetry of a regular pentagon, the 5 field vectors point radially outward from center, equally spaced at 72° angles.
The vector sum of 5 equally spaced unit vectors is zero, so $\vec{E} = 0$.

subject: Physics
q: Two equal positive point charges are separated by a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>a</mi></math>. The distance of a point from the centre of the line joining two charges on the equatorial line (perpendicular bisector) at which force experienced by a test charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> becomes maximum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>a</mi><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
question: Two equal positive point charges are separated by a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>a</mi></math>. The distance of a point from the centre of the line joining two charges on the equatorial line (perpendicular bisector) at which force experienced by a test charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> becomes maximum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>a</mi><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
Let us assume at distance y, force is maximum.

Now, force at a distance y is given by, Fnet=2Fcosθ

So, Fnet=2Kqq0yy2+a232

For Fnet to be maximum, dFnetdy=0

Or Kqq0y2+a232-y32×2yy2+a212y2+a23=0

Simplifying, we get y=a2.

Hence, the value of x=2.

subject: Physics
q: Considering a group of positive charges, which of the following statements is correct?
question: Considering a group of positive charges, which of the following statements is correct?
options: Net potential of the system cannot be zero at a point but net electric field can be zero at that point
Net potential of the system at a point can be zero but net electric field can't be zero at that point
Both the net potential and the net field can be zero at a point
Both the net potential and the net electric field cannot be zero at a point
solution:
As we know, net potential will be algebraic sum of potential due to each charge, so we can write Vnet=∑kQiri.

As given in the question, Qi and ri are positive, so

∴  Vnet>0

But electric field is a vector quantity, so it can be zero for a given arrangement of charges. For example, electric field at the centre of the two equal charges at certain distance will be zero as electric field due to both charges will be in opposite direction and hence will cancel each other.

subject: Physics
q: A long cylindrical volume contains a uniformly distributed charge of density ρ C m-3. The electric field inside the cylindrical volume at a distance x=2ε0ρ m from its axis is _____ V m-1.
question: A long cylindrical volume contains a uniformly distributed charge of density ρ C m-3. The electric field inside the cylindrical volume at a distance x=2ε0ρ m from its axis is _____ V m-1.
solution:
Given here, Volume charge density =ρ C m-3.

Consider a Gaussian cylindrical surface of height h and radius x.

Let q charge is enclosed by the cylinder of radius x.

From Gauss's law,

∫EdScos0=qε0

Here, q=volume charge density×volume of imaginary cylinder.

 ⇒E2πxh=ρ×πx2hε0

⇒E=ρx2ε0  

⇒E=ρ2ε0×2ε0ρ=1

Thus, electric field is 1 V m-1.

subject: Physics
q: Two charges $7 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and $-2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ are placed at $(-9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ and $(9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ respectively in an external field $E=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{r^{2}} \hat{r}$, where $A=9 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}. \mathrm{m}^{2}$. Considering the potential at infinity is 0, the electrostatic energy of the configuration is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
question: Two charges $7 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and $-2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ are placed at $(-9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ and $(9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ respectively in an external field $E=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{r^{2}} \hat{r}$, where $A=9 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}. \mathrm{m}^{2}$. Considering the potential at infinity is 0, the electrostatic energy of the configuration is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
solution:
The total electrostatic energy $U$ of a system of two charges $q_1$ and $q_2$ in an external field is given by $U = q_1 V(r_1) + q_2 V(r_2) + \frac{k q_1 q_2}{r_{12}}$.
Given external electric field $E = \frac{A}{r^2} \hat{r}$, the potential $V(r)$ is found by $V(r) = -\int_{\infty}^{r} E \cdot dr = -\int_{\infty}^{r} \frac{A}{r^2} dr = \frac{A}{r}$.
Parameters given:
$q_1 = 7 \mu C = 7 \times 10^{-6} C$ at $r_1 = 9 cm = 0.09 m$.
$q_2 = -2 \mu C = -2 \times 10^{-6} C$ at $r_2 = 9 cm = 0.09 m$.
Distance between charges $r_{12} = 9 - (-9) = 18 cm = 0.18 m$.
$A = 9 \times 10^5 N/C \cdot m^2$.
Calculating individual terms:
$q_1 V(r_1) = q_1 \frac{A}{r_1} = (7 \times 10^{-6}) \frac{9 \times 10^5}{0.09} = 7 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^7 = 70 J$.
$q_2 V(r_2) = q_2 \frac{A}{r_2} = (-2 \times 10^{-6}) \frac{9 \times 10^5}{0.09} = -2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^7 = -20 J$.
Interaction energy $\frac{k q_1 q_2}{r_{12}} = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times (7 \times 10^{-6}) \times (-2 \times 10^{-6})}{0.18} = \frac{-126 \times 10^{-3}}{0.18} = -0.7 J$.
Total energy $U = 70 - 20 - 0.7 = 49.3 J$.

q: Charges -q and +q, located at A and B, respectively, constitute an electric dipole. Distance AB=2a, O is the mid point of the dipole and OP is perpendicular to AB. A charge Q is placed at P where OP=y and y≫2a. The charge Q experiences an electrostatic force F. If Q is now moved along the equatorial line to P' such that OP'=y3 the force on Q will be close toy3≪2a
question: Charges -q and +q, located at A and B, respectively, constitute an electric dipole. Distance AB=2a, O is the mid point of the dipole and OP is perpendicular to AB. A charge Q is placed at P where OP=y and y≫2a. The charge Q experiences an electrostatic force F. If Q is now moved along the equatorial line to P' such that OP'=y3 the force on Q will be close toy3≪2a
solution:
Electric field intensity at the distance y along the perpendicular bisector is

E1=KPy3

So, force at distance y on Q,

F=Q(E1)=QKPy3 ...(1)

F'=QKPy33 ...(2)

Dividing 1 by 2, we get,

F'F=33

∴ F'=27F

q: A positive charge particle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mg</mi></math> is thrown in opposite direction to a uniform electric field of strength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the charge on the particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and the initial velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, how much distance it will travel before coming to the rest momentarily
question: A positive charge particle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mg</mi></math> is thrown in opposite direction to a uniform electric field of strength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the charge on the particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and the initial velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, how much distance it will travel before coming to the rest momentarily
solution:
Force experienced by a particle with charge q in an electric field F=qE.

Now, the acceleration produced is given as a=Fm=qEm=40×10-6×1051×10-4

=4×104 m  s-2  (As the particle is projected against the electric field, hence it is decelerated)

Using, v2=u2-2as

We have, 02=u2-2as

s=v22a=20022×4×104=0.5 m

subject: Physics
q: A rigid dipole undergoes a simple harmonic motion about its centre in the presence of an electric field $\vec{E}_1=E_0\hat{x}$. If another electric field $\vec{E}_2=2E_0(\hat{y}+\hat{z})$ is introduced to the system, what will be the percentage change in the frequency of the oscillation (approximate)?
question: A rigid dipole undergoes a simple harmonic motion about its centre in the presence of an electric field $\vec{E}_1=E_0\hat{x}$. If another electric field $\vec{E}_2=2E_0(\hat{y}+\hat{z})$ is introduced to the system, what will be the percentage change in the frequency of the oscillation (approximate)?
solution:
The frequency of oscillation of a rigid dipole in an electric field is given by $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{pE}{I}}$, where $p$ is the dipole moment, $E$ is the magnitude of the electric field, and $I$ is the moment of inertia.

This implies $f \propto \sqrt{E}$.

The initial electric field is $\vec{E}_1 = E_0 \hat{x}$, so its magnitude is $E_1 = E_0$.

The initial frequency is $f_1 \propto \sqrt{E_0}$.

When the second electric field $\vec{E}_2 = 2E_0(\hat{y} + \hat{z})$ is introduced, the net electric field becomes $\vec{E}_{net} = \vec{E}_1 + \vec{E}_2 = E_0 \hat{x} + 2E_0 \hat{y} + 2E_0 \hat{z}$.

The magnitude of the net electric field is $E_{net} = \sqrt{E_0^2 + (2E_0)^2 + (2E_0)^2} = \sqrt{E_0^2 + 4E_0^2 + 4E_0^2} = 3E_0$.

The new frequency is $f_2 \propto \sqrt{3E_0}$.

The percentage change in the frequency is given by $\dfrac{f_2 - f_1}{f_1} \times 100\%$.

Substituting the values, we get $\left( \dfrac{\sqrt{3E_0}}{\sqrt{E_0}} - 1 \right) \times 100\% = (\sqrt{3} - 1) \times 100\%$.

Using $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$, the percentage change is $(1.732 - 1) \times 100\% = 73.2\%$.

The approximate percentage change is $73\%$.

Answer: $73\%$

q:
A small bob of mass 100 mg and charge $+10 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is connected to an insulating string of length 1 m. It is brought near to an infinitely long nonconducting sheet of charge density ' $\sigma$ ' as shown in figure. If string subtends an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the sheet at equilibrium the charge density of sheet will be :
(Given, $\varepsilon_0=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \frac{\mathrm{~F}}{\mathrm{~m}}$ and acceleration due to gravity, $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$)
question:
A small bob of mass 100 mg and charge $+10 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is connected to an insulating string of length 1 m. It is brought near to an infinitely long nonconducting sheet of charge density ' $\sigma$ ' as shown in figure. If string subtends an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the sheet at equilibrium the charge density of sheet will be :
(Given, $\varepsilon_0=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \frac{\mathrm{~F}}{\mathrm{~m}}$ and acceleration due to gravity, $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{qE}=\mathrm{mg} \\ & \mathrm{q}\left[\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}\right]=\mathrm{mg} \\ & \sigma=\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{mg}}{\mathrm{q}} \\ & \sigma=\frac{2 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 10}{10 \times 10^{-6}} \\ & \sigma=17.7 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}^2 \\ & \sigma=1.77 \mathrm{nC} / \mathrm{m}^2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Inside uniformly charged spherical shell, $E=0$
$\therefore \mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{III}$

For uniformly charged infinite plate
$E=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}$
$\therefore \mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{II}$

Outside of spherical shell
$E=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_2}=\frac{\sigma R^2}{\varepsilon_0 r^2}$
$\therefore \mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{IV}$

Between two plates $E=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}$
$\therefore \mathrm{D} \rightarrow \mathrm{I}$

question: A point charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is placed at origin. Another point charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is placed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. Charge of proton is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. The proton is placed on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis so that the electrostatic force on the proton in zero. In this situation, the position of the proton from the origin is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
Let at point P net electric field is zero.

As direction of electric field due to both charges at point P is opposite, equating magnitude we get

14πε04q0x2=14πε0q0x-122⇒2x-12=x⇒x=24 cm

Distance of proton from origin =24 cm.

q: Two identical conducting spheres <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> carry an equal charges. question: Two identical conducting spheres <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> carry an equal charges. They are separated by a distance much larger than their diameters, and the force between them is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>. A third identical conducting sphere, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math>, is uncharged. Sphere <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> is first touched to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, then to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, and then removed. As a result, the force between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> would be equal to:
solution:
F=kq2r2 when A and C are touched charge on both will be q2. Then when B and C are touched, qB= q2+q2=3q4
F′=kqAqBr2= k ×q2 ×3q4r2=38kq2r2=38F.
